title: SEGMENT 5 — Mouse ETX embryoids: Zernicka-Goetz pushes past the blastocyst

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — Mouse ETX embryoids: Zernicka-Goetz pushes past the blastocyst

While Rivron's blastoids were establishing the basic recipe at the blastocyst stage, another lab was doing something even more ambitious: trying to build embryo-like structures that pushed past implantation, through the early post-implantation stages, into what's called the egg cylinder or gastrula-like stage.
The lab was Magdalena Zernicka-Goetz's, then at the University of Cambridge, now also at Caltech.

Zernicka-Goetz had spent her career studying mouse pre- and peri-implantation development, and she had a deep intuition for how the three early lineages — epiblast (which becomes the body), trophoblast (placenta), and primitive endoderm (yolk sac) — talk to each other to coordinate the next steps of development.

She thought that if you put all three lineages together as stem cells, in the right environment, you could get something that went beyond the blastocyst stage and into early post-implantation morphogenesis.
Her group called these constructs ETX embryos — E for embryonic stem cells, T for trophoblast stem cells, X for extraembryonic endoderm (XEN) cells.
ETX.
Three letters, three lineages, one structure.

In a series of papers starting around 2017 and culminating in 2022, Zernicka-Goetz's group showed that you could combine mouse ES cells, mouse TS cells, and mouse XEN cells in defined ratios, in a 3D matrix, and get them to self-organize into structures that recapitulated not just the blastocyst stage but the post-implantation egg cylinder stage — the elongated, cup-shaped early mouse embryo that forms after implantation, with epiblast in one half, extraembryonic tissue in the other, primitive
endoderm wrapping around the outside, a pro-amniotic cavity opening up in the middle, and the beginnings of the anterior-posterior axis being established.
This was a major step beyond Rivron's blastoids.
A blastoid is a static snapshot of an early stage — it implants, but it doesn't develop in any sustained way.
An ETX embryoid is a developing model.
It changes shape over time.
It establishes axes.
It begins gastrulation-like processes.
It expresses the molecular markers of an early post-implantation embryo.

In the most advanced versions, ETX embryoids have shown signs of beginning the primitive streak, which is the morphological hallmark of gastrulation — the moment when the three definitive germ layers (endoderm, mesoderm, ectoderm) begin to form.
The recipe, like Rivron's, depended on getting cell ratios right, getting the gel right (typically Matrigel or a defined alternative), and getting the signaling cocktail right.
Different versions of the protocol used different combinations of small molecules and culture conditions.
Yields were low in early iterations — only a small fraction of starting aggregates would develop into a proper ETX embryoid — but they could be selected morphologically and analyzed in detail.
The biological insight was important.
The three lineages aren't optional ornaments around the epiblast.
They're necessary instructors.
The trophoblast and the primitive endoderm send signals to the epiblast that tell it when and where to form the primitive streak, where the anterior is, where the posterior is.
Without the extraembryonic lineages, the epiblast doesn't know what to do.
ETX embryoids work because they restore the full conversation.
Embryonic + trophoblast + extraembryonic endoderm, talking to each other, give you something that goes beyond a clump and starts to behave like a developing embryo.
The Zernicka-Goetz work also raised the bar on what counted as a "synthetic embryo.
" Rivron's blastoids were impressive but limited — a snapshot at a single stage.
ETX embryoids were a process — a stem cell-derived structure that develops over time, that changes, that begins to lay down the body plan.

This is closer to what a real embryo does, and it raised, more acutely than blastoids did, the question: at what point of developmental progression does a stem cell aggregate become, ethically and scientifically, an embryo?
We'll come back to this question in detail.
For now, hold the picture: ETX embryoids in mouse, by 2022, were getting through implantation-stage and into early gastrulation-like organization.

They weren't going all the way to a beating heart and a brain — that was a different lab, which we'll meet in Segment 7 — but they were demonstrating that the recipe could carry you well past the blastocyst, into the early body plan stages.
The Zernicka-Goetz lab has also been important in another way.
They've been outspoken about the ethical and policy implications of their work.

Zernicka-Goetz has written and spoken extensively about the need for new ethical frameworks for synthetic embryos — frameworks that don't simply transplant the rules made for real embryos, because synthetic embryos are different in important ways, but also frameworks that don't pretend the ethical questions don't exist, because they clearly do.
Her position has been roughly: this work is too important to stop, but too consequential to let proceed without scrutiny.
We'll come back to that position when we discuss the 2021 ISSCR guidelines.
